chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Hi guys, my name is Ritesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: In the last video, we took an intuition of Multilayer Perceptron

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: We tried to understand how Multilayer Perceptrons work and why they can work

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: In the end, I gave you some examples on Tensorflow Playground

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, slowly we will try to understand Multilayer Perceptron in a deeper way

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: To understand Multilayer Perceptron, the most difficult thing is to train the algorithm

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: which is called Back Propagation

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, everyone who has worked in deep learning knows that it is very difficult to understand Back Propagation

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: I have also experienced it

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And I think the biggest reason for it is that beginners make a mistake

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And that mistake is that when you make a neural network

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: then many weights and biases come in that neural network

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, if we don't learn to denote those weights and biases properly

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: If we don't learn their notation, then while doing Back Propagation, there is a lot of confusion

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, I made this mistake and I don't want you to make this mistake

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: That's why in today's video, our whole goal would be actually two things

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: First, I want to teach you that by looking at any neural network architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: First of all, you should be able to calculate the number of trainable parameters in it

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The number of trainable parameters means that when you train that model

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: then what is the total number of weights and biases you have to find the value of

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, the first thing we will learn in today's video is that if you are being given a neural network architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: then you should calculate it quickly that there are so many weights and biases in it

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The second thing we will learn is that how do you denote those weights and biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: What do you name them? We have to learn their notation

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And we will learn a standard notation which is followed in the industry

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, what will happen is that in the future when you are studying a difficult algorithm like Back Propagation

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: then you will never get confused by looking at any weight that which layer is this weight

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, that's the whole idea of this video. It is a small video but trust me it is very important

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Please don't skip this one. So, that's it for the introduction. Let's start the video

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, guys let's start. First of all, let me explain you the setup

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: What you are seeing on the screen is the neural network architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: I will explain you the whole notation by using this architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, there are 4 layers in total. Yes, 4 layers are there

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, there is an input layer which you are calling layer 0

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Hidden layer 1 is layer 1, hidden layer 2 is layer 2 and output layer is layer 3

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Also, the data that we have here is 4 dimensional data

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: You can see where M denotes the number of rows and the value of N is 4

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, if you have data it will look like this

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: There would be column 1, column 2, column 3, column 4 and finally your output column

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, what I am doing is that I will insert row by row data here

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, x i1 is the value of the first column of the ith row

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: This is the value of the ith row and this is the value of the ith row

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, when I will provide one row I will insert these values here

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, the question is that how many trainable parameters do you have?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: This is the first question that how many trainable parameters do you have?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Trainable parameters means that when you will train on it

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, your back propagation algorithm will find out the value of weights and biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, you should be able to calculate this by looking at the neural network architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, let me show you

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: If you focus here, let's count the number of weights and biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, clearly if you see the combination here, there are 4 nodes and 3 nodes

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, 4 multiplied by 3, there are 12 weights

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: You can see, if you count all these weights, there are 12 weights

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Also, one more thing, if you see this color encoding, I have created these weights in such a way

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: that all the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are encoded in one color

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, all the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in black color

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: All the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in red color

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: All the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in green color

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Also, I have used the same color encoding so that you can easily understand

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, coming back, we have to count the total number of weights and biases in this neural network

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, here there are 12 weights and its bias, its bias and its bias

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, we have 3 biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, if we come here, there are 3 and 2 here

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, we will have total weights, 6 weights

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And then its bias and its bias, 2 biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: After that, if we come here, we will have 2 weights

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: We can understand by counting and then its bias

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, here total is 15

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Here total is 8

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And here total is 3

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: If we add these 3, 15 plus 8, 23 plus 3

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: There are 26 trainable parameters

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: This means that when you train this neural network

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, your back propagation algorithm will try to find out the value of 26 weights and biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: You should always understand this by looking at the neural network architecture

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, coming back to the original question

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: How do you denote weights and biases in multilayer perceptron?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: What is the notation?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: We will solve this now

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Actually, I will show you 3 things

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: I will show you how to denote weights, how to denote biases and how to denote outputs

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The output coming out of this node is the input for the next node

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, it is important to know that

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, we will see the notation of output

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, let's start with biases because they are the easiest

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, the notation to denote bias is Bij

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Where i represents the layer number

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And j represents the node number in that layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: For example, if you talk about this node

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, its bias will be 1,1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is in layer number 1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And it is the first node of layer number 1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Similarly, it will be 1,2

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is the second node in the first layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: It will be 1,3

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is the third node in the first layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: If you talk about this node

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: It will be 2,1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is the first node in the second layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: It will be 2,2

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is the second node in the second layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And it will be 3,1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Because it is the first node in the third layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, that is how you denote biases

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, the question arises

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: How will you denote the output?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: It is very simple to denote the output

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Again, you use Oij

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Which is exactly the same

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: i represents the layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And j represents the node number

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, don't think too much

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The output of any node

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Follows the same notation

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Which denotes the bias

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: For example, if you talk about this node

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, it produces two outputs

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: This and this

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The output of these two will be O,1,1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Ok

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: This node produces two outputs

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: The output of these two will be O,1,2

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And two of these are producing

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: One of these is producing

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: One of these is producing

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: One of these is producing O,3,1

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: And O,3,1 is your prediction

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Ok

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, that is how you denote the output

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Now, the last and the most tricky thing

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: How to denote weights?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: To denote weights

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: You need three things

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: i, j and k

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Ok

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: So, what does k mean?

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: It means the weight

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Which is entering which layer

chunks.title: MLP Notation
chunks.text: Ok

chunks.title: MLP Notation

chunks.title: MLP Notation

text: Hi guys, my name is Ritesh and you are welcome to my YouTube channel In the last video, we took an intuition of Multilayer Perceptron We tried to understand how Multilayer Perceptrons work and why they can work In the end, I gave you some examples on Tensorflow Playground Now, slowly we will try to understand Multilayer Perceptron in a deeper way To understand Multilayer Perceptron, the most difficult thing is to train the algorithm which is called Back Propagation So, everyone who has worked in deep learning knows that it is very difficult to understand Back Propagation I have also experienced it And I think the biggest reason for it is that beginners make a mistake And that mistake is that when you make a neural network then many weights and biases come in that neural network So, if we don't learn to denote those weights and biases properly If we don't learn their notation, then while doing Back Propagation, there is a lot of confusion So, I made this mistake and I don't want you to make this mistake That's why in today's video, our whole goal would be actually two things First, I want to teach you that by looking at any neural network architecture First of all, you should be able to calculate the number of trainable parameters in it The number of trainable parameters means that when you train that model then what is the total number of weights and biases you have to find the value of So, the first thing we will learn in today's video is that if you are being given a neural network architecture then you should calculate it quickly that there are so many weights and biases in it The second thing we will learn is that how do you denote those weights and biases What do you name them? We have to learn their notation And we will learn a standard notation which is followed in the industry So, what will happen is that in the future when you are studying a difficult algorithm like Back Propagation then you will never get confused by looking at any weight that which layer is this weight So, that's the whole idea of this video. It is a small video but trust me it is very important Please don't skip this one. So, that's it for the introduction. Let's start the video So, guys let's start. First of all, let me explain you the setup What you are seeing on the screen is the neural network architecture I will explain you the whole notation by using this architecture So, there are 4 layers in total. Yes, 4 layers are there So, there is an input layer which you are calling layer 0 Hidden layer 1 is layer 1, hidden layer 2 is layer 2 and output layer is layer 3 Also, the data that we have here is 4 dimensional data You can see where M denotes the number of rows and the value of N is 4 So, if you have data it will look like this There would be column 1, column 2, column 3, column 4 and finally your output column So, what I am doing is that I will insert row by row data here So, x i1 is the value of the first column of the ith row This is the value of the ith row and this is the value of the ith row So, when I will provide one row I will insert these values here Now, the question is that how many trainable parameters do you have? This is the first question that how many trainable parameters do you have? Trainable parameters means that when you will train on it So, your back propagation algorithm will find out the value of weights and biases So, you should be able to calculate this by looking at the neural network architecture So, let me show you If you focus here, let's count the number of weights and biases So, clearly if you see the combination here, there are 4 nodes and 3 nodes So, 4 multiplied by 3, there are 12 weights You can see, if you count all these weights, there are 12 weights Also, one more thing, if you see this color encoding, I have created these weights in such a way that all the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are encoded in one color So, all the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in black color All the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in red color All the weights that you are getting from the previous layer are in green color Also, I have used the same color encoding so that you can easily understand So, coming back, we have to count the total number of weights and biases in this neural network So, here there are 12 weights and its bias, its bias and its bias So, we have 3 biases Now, if we come here, there are 3 and 2 here So, we will have total weights, 6 weights And then its bias and its bias, 2 biases After that, if we come here, we will have 2 weights We can understand by counting and then its bias So, here total is 15 Here total is 8 And here total is 3 If we add these 3, 15 plus 8, 23 plus 3 There are 26 trainable parameters This means that when you train this neural network So, your back propagation algorithm will try to find out the value of 26 weights and biases You should always understand this by looking at the neural network architecture Now, coming back to the original question How do you denote weights and biases in multilayer perceptron? What is the notation? We will solve this now Actually, I will show you 3 things I will show you how to denote weights, how to denote biases and how to denote outputs The output coming out of this node is the input for the next node So, it is important to know that So, we will see the notation of output So, let's start with biases because they are the easiest So, the notation to denote bias is Bij Where i represents the layer number And j represents the node number in that layer For example, if you talk about this node So, its bias will be 1,1 Because it is in layer number 1 And it is the first node of layer number 1 Similarly, it will be 1,2 Because it is the second node in the first layer It will be 1,3 Because it is the third node in the first layer If you talk about this node It will be 2,1 Because it is the first node in the second layer It will be 2,2 Because it is the second node in the second layer And it will be 3,1 Because it is the first node in the third layer So, that is how you denote biases Now, the question arises How will you denote the output? It is very simple to denote the output Again, you use Oij Which is exactly the same i represents the layer And j represents the node number So, don't think too much The output of any node Follows the same notation Which denotes the bias For example, if you talk about this node So, it produces two outputs This and this The output of these two will be O,1,1 Ok This node produces two outputs The output of these two will be O,1,2 And two of these are producing O,1,3 One of these is producing O,2,1 One of these is producing O,2,2 One of these is producing O,3,1 And O,3,1 is your prediction Ok So, that is how you denote the output Now, the last and the most tricky thing How to denote weights? To denote weights You need three things i, j and k Ok So, what does k mean? It means the weight Which is entering which layer Ok Remember i means Which node is producing Which number in the current layer And j means Which node is producing Which number in the current layer I
